critic_raw:
The category embeddings from a dictionary of derogatory words create an implicit lexical feature that may dominate the multi-task learning signal. If the model relies primarily on derogatory word presence rather than semantic context, SKS is effectively a lexicon lookup with a neural wrapper. The ablation removing category embeddings while keeping the multi-task setup is the critical test for whether the attention architecture and sentiment sharing add anything beyond lexicon matching — this test is not described.

The two benchmark datasets may capture different demographic targeting patterns (racial hate speech vs. gender-based hate speech vs. political hate speech). If SKS learns demographic-specific derogatory vocabulary from one dataset, its performance on the other may reflect vocabulary overlap rather than generalization of the hate speech detection mechanism.

Multi-task learning with sentiment analysis assumes hate speech and sentiment are compatible tasks with shared representations. This assumption breaks in two common cases: neutral-register dehumanizing language (hate speech without negative sentiment, e.g., clinical-sounding supremacist text) and hyperbolic non-hateful content (strong negative sentiment without hate speech). The degree to which these cases appear in the benchmark datasets determines whether the multi-task setup helps or hurts, and this is not analyzed.
all_issues_raised: Category embeddings from a derogatory word dictionary may dominate the model, making SKS effectively a lexicon lookup; the critical ablation removing category embeddings is not described.

The two benchmark datasets may have different demographic targeting patterns; performance on the second dataset may reflect vocabulary overlap with the first rather than generalization of the hate speech detection mechanism.

The multi-task sentiment assumption breaks for neutral-register dehumanizing text and hyperbolic non-hateful content; whether these cases are present in the benchmarks and how they affect multi-task learning is not analyzed.
all_issues_adjudicated: Category embeddings from a derogatory word dictionary may dominate the model, making SKS effectively a lexicon lookup; the critical ablation removing category embeddings is not described.

The two benchmark datasets may have different demographic targeting patterns; performance on the second dataset may reflect vocabulary overlap with the first rather than generalization of the hate speech detection mechanism.

The multi-task sentiment assumption breaks for neutral-register dehumanizing text and hyperbolic non-hateful content; whether these cases are present in the benchmarks and how they affect multi-task learning is not analyzed.
